title: Declarations are grouped by indentation
rule: Declarations with the same indentation belong to the same group. More-indented lines belong to the preceding declaration, and less-indented lines terminate the current group.
rationale: Cryptol uses layout-sensitive syntax, so incorrect indentation can change declaration grouping or terminate a block unexpectedly.
positive_example:
f x = x + y + z
  where
  y = x * x
  z = x + y

g y = y
negative_example:
f x = x + y + z
where
  y = x * x
  z = x + y
g y = y
constraints: Layout applies at the top level and inside where blocks.
retrieval_title: basic_syntax / layout / Declarations are grouped by indentation
retrieval_text:
title: Declarations are grouped by indentation
topic: basic_syntax
subtopic: layout
keywords: layout | indentation | where
rule: Declarations with the same indentation belong to the same group. More-indented lines belong to the preceding declaration, and less-indented lines terminate the current group.
constraints: Layout applies at the top level and inside where blocks.
applicable_when: writing multiple declarations | writing where blocks
positive_example:
f x = x + y + z
  where
  y = x * x
  z = x + y

g y = y
negative_example:
f x = x + y + z
where
  y = x * x
  z = x + y
g y = y

title: Cryptol supports nested block comments and line comments
rule: Use `/* ... */` for block comments and `//` for line comments. Block comments may be nested.
rationale: Comment syntax is stable and useful for preserving annotations without changing semantics.
positive_example:
/* outer /* nested */ still valid */
// one-line comment
constraints: Block comments must be properly closed.
retrieval_title: basic_syntax / comments / Cryptol supports nested block comments and line comments

topic: basic_syntax
subtopic: comments

rule: Use `/* ... */` for block comments and `//` for line comments. Block comments may be nested.
constraints: Block comments must be properly closed.
applicable_when: adding comments to generated code | preserving notes from source algorithms
positive_example:
/* outer /* nested */ still valid */
// one-line comment
negative_example:
/* unterminated block comment

title: Docstrings use comments starting with exactly two asterisks
rule: Documentation comments attached to declarations use block comments starting with exactly `/** ... */`.
rationale: This syntax is special in Cryptol and enables REPL help and docstring test checking.
positive_example:
/** Example documentation for x */
x = 1
negative_example:
/* regular comment, not a docstring */
x = 1
constraints: Docstring code fences checked by `:check-docstrings` are unlabeled or labeled `repl`.

constraints: Docstring code fences checked by `:check-docstrings` are unlabeled or labeled `repl`.
applicable_when: generating documented libraries | writing property docs and examples
positive_example:
/** Example documentation for x */
x = 1
negative_example:
/* regular comment, not a docstring */
x = 1

title: Identifiers must start with a letter or underscore
rule: An identifier must start with an English letter or underscore. Later characters may be letters, digits, underscores, or primes (`'`).
rationale: Using unsupported identifier forms causes parse failures.
positive_example:
name
name1
name'
longer_name
_Name
constraints: Reserved keywords cannot be used as programmer-defined identifiers.

rule: An identifier must start with an English letter or underscore. Later characters may be letters, digits, underscores, or primes (`'`).
constraints: Reserved keywords cannot be used as programmer-defined identifiers.
applicable_when: naming functions | naming local bindings | naming helper definitions
positive_example:
name
name1
name'
longer_name
_Name
negative_example:
1name
name-with-dash

title: Reserved keywords cannot be reused as user-defined names
rule: Identifiers with special meaning in Cryptol, such as `module`, `where`, `type`, `property`, and `import`, must not be used as programmer-defined names.
rationale: Reserved names collide with the parser and module/type system.
positive_example: helper = 1
constraints: This applies to all reserved keywords and built-in syntactic identifiers.

constraints: This applies to all reserved keywords and built-in syntactic identifiers.
applicable_when: naming declarations | naming generated helpers
positive_example:
helper = 1
negative_example:
where = 1
module = 2

title: Infix operators follow Cryptol precedence and associativity rules
rule: Infix expressions are parsed according to the built-in precedence table. For example, `*` binds tighter than `+`, and unary prefix operators are parsed with their own precedence.
rationale: Misunderstanding precedence changes semantics and often leads to incorrect generated code.
constraints: Use parentheses whenever operator grouping could be ambiguous to the generator or reader.
retrieval_title: basic_syntax / operator_precedence / Infix operators follow Cryptol precedence and associativity rules
retrieval_text:

subtopic: operator_precedence
keywords: precedence | associativity | + | * | @ | #
rule: Infix expressions are parsed according to the built-in precedence table. For example, `*` binds tighter than `+`, and unary prefix operators are parsed with their own precedence.
constraints: Use parentheses whenever operator grouping could be ambiguous to the generator or reader.
applicable_when: generating arithmetic expressions | combining sequence operators with arithmetic
positive_example:
2 + 3 * 5
negative_example:
Assume `2 + 3 * 5` means `(2 + 3) * 5`.

title: Binary, octal, and hexadecimal literals have fixed bit widths
rule: Binary, octal, and hexadecimal literals produce fixed-width bit sequences whose widths are determined by the number of digits and base. Decimal literals are overloaded and depend on context.
rationale: Literal width matters heavily in Cryptol because words are width-indexed types.
negative_example: Assume `0xF` and decimal `15` always have the same inferred type without context.
constraints: Use explicit type context when decimal literal width matters.
retrieval_title: basic_syntax / numeric_literals / Binary, octal, and hexadecimal literals have fixed bit widths
retrieval_text:

subtopic: numeric_literals
keywords: numeric literals | 0b | 0o | 0x | width

constraints: Use explicit type context when decimal literal width matters.
applicable_when: choosing constants for bitvector code | writing width-sensitive formulas
positive_example:
0b1010      // [4]
0o1234      // [12]
0x1234      // [16]
10          // overloaded
negative_example:
Assume `0xF` and decimal `15` always have the same inferred type without context.

title: Type signatures can quantify type variables and constraints
rule: A type signature may quantify type parameters in braces and place constraints before `=>`, for example `f : {a,b} (fin a) => [a] b`.
rationale: Well-formed signatures are essential for width-indexed and polymorphic Cryptol code.
positive_example: f,g : {a,b} (fin a) => [a] b
constraints: Constraints precede `=>` and refer to the quantified type variables.

constraints: Constraints precede `=>` and refer to the quantified type variables.
applicable_when: writing top-level signatures | making generated helpers polymorphic
positive_example:
f,g : {a,b} (fin a) => [a] b
negative_example:
f : [a] b => fin a

title: Numeric constraint guards dispatch on type-level numeric constraints
rule: A declaration with a signature may use numeric constraint guards to select behavior based on numeric type parameters. Each branch starts with `|` and states constraints on numeric parameters.
rationale: Constraint guards enable type-directed branching that ordinary value-level `if` does not provide.
positive_example:
len : {n, a} (fin n) => [n]a -> Integer
len xs | n > 0  => 1 + len (drop `{1} xs)
       | n == 0 => 0
negative_example:
len' : {n, a} (fin n) => [n]a -> Integer
len' xs = if `n > 0 then 1 + len' (drop `{1} xs) else 0
constraints: Guards only support numeric-literal constraints.
Guards should be exhaustive.
Each guard is checked independently.

constraints: Guards only support numeric-literal constraints. | Guards should be exhaustive. | Each guard is checked independently.
applicable_when: encoding recursive length-dependent functions | branching on numeric type parameters
positive_example:
len : {n, a} (fin n) => [n]a -> Integer
len xs | n > 0  => 1 + len (drop `{1} xs)
       | n == 0 => 0
negative_example:
len' : {n, a} (fin n) => [n]a -> Integer
len' xs = if `n > 0 then 1 + len' (drop `{1} xs) else 0

title: Function application is space-separated and left-associative
rule: Call functions by writing the function followed by its arguments separated by spaces, for example `g x y`. Parentheses are required when a single argument is itself a tuple or when parsing would otherwise change meaning.
rationale: Application syntax interacts with prefix operators and tuples, so omitted parentheses can change arity or parse structure.
positive_example:
f 2
g x y
h (x,y)
constraints: Use parentheses around tuple arguments and around negative literals when needed.

constraints: Use parentheses around tuple arguments and around negative literals when needed.
applicable_when: generating function calls | passing tuples as single arguments
positive_example:
f 2
g x y
h (x,y)
negative_example:
h x,y

title: Unary minus interacts with function application and may require parentheses
rule: Unary `-` is a prefix operator and may bind over a following application. Use parentheses when you intend a negative literal or a negated subexpression as a single function argument.
rationale: Expressions like `f (-2)` and `- f 2` mean different things.
constraints: When in doubt, parenthesize negative arguments explicitly.
applicable_when: writing arithmetic expressions with negative values

constraints: When in doubt, parenthesize negative arguments explicitly.
applicable_when: writing arithmetic expressions with negative values | passing negative values to functions
positive_example:
f (-2)
- f 2
negative_example:
f -2

title: Type annotations bind to the nearest expression or pattern
rule: An explicit type annotation applies to the nearest expression, pattern, or argument position where it is written, not necessarily to the larger surrounding expression.
rationale: Misplaced annotations are a common source of incorrect assumptions in generated code.
negative_example:
\x -> x : [8]    // annotates `x`, not the whole lambda
if x then y else z : Bit  // annotates `z`, not the whole if
constraints: Parenthesize if you need the type annotation to apply to a larger expression.

subtopic: type_annotations

constraints: Parenthesize if you need the type annotation to apply to a larger expression.
applicable_when: fixing type errors | adding explicit width annotations
positive_example:
f (x : [8]) = x + 1
2 + 3 : [8]
negative_example:
\x -> x : [8]    // annotates `x`, not the whole lambda
if x then y else z : Bit  // annotates `z`, not the whole if

title: Polymorphic definitions may be instantiated with named type arguments
rule: A polymorphic value may be explicitly instantiated by passing named type arguments using backtick-brace syntax, for example `f `{ tyParam = 13 }`.
rationale: Explicit instantiation is useful when inference is insufficient or ambiguous.
constraints: Type argument names must match quantified type parameter names.

positive_example:
f : { tyParam } tyParam
f = zero

f `{ tyParam = 13 }
negative_example:
f { tyParam = 13 }

title: Local declarations introduced with where have the weakest precedence
rule: A trailing `where` block binds to the whole preceding expression and has the weakest precedence among expressions.
rationale: This affects scope and parsing of large expressions with local helpers and local type synonyms.
positive_example:
2 + x : [T]
  where
  type T = 8
  x      = 2
negative_example:
\y -> x + y
  where x = 2   // `y` is not in scope inside the definition of `x`
constraints: Local declarations are scope-delimited by layout.
By default, local bindings without signatures are monomorphized.

applicable_when: using local helper bindings | introducing local type synonyms
positive_example:
2 + x : [T]
  where
  type T = 8
  x      = 2
negative_example:
\y -> x + y
  where x = 2   // `y` is not in scope inside the definition of `x`

title: If-expressions and lambdas may be used as final block arguments without parentheses
rule: When used as the last argument to a function call, `if` expressions and lambdas do not need parentheses.
rationale: This is a useful surface-syntax feature that appears often in hand-written Cryptol.
positive_example:
f \x -> x
2 + if x
      then y
      else z
negative_example: Assume all final lambda or if arguments must always be parenthesized.
constraints: This applies specifically when the block form is the final argument in the call.

constraints: This applies specifically when the block form is the final argument in the call.
applicable_when: generating readable higher-order code | passing lambdas inline
positive_example:
f \x -> x
2 + if x
      then y
      else z
negative_example:
Assume all final lambda or if arguments must always be parenthesized.

title: If-then-else supports multiple guarded branches
rule: Cryptol supports multi-way conditionals using `if ... then ... | ... then ... else ...`.
rationale: This gives a compact alternative to deeply nested conditionals for value-level branching.
positive_example:
x = if y % 2 == 0 then 1
     | y % 3 == 0 then 2
     | y % 5 == 0 then 3
     else 7
negative_example: if y % 2 == 0 then 1 | y % 3 == 0 then 2   // missing final else
constraints: A value-level conditional must include a final `else` branch.
applicable_when: encoding case-like value branching without enums

constraints: A value-level conditional must include a final `else` branch.
applicable_when: encoding case-like value branching without enums | writing arithmetic conditionals
positive_example:
x = if y % 2 == 0 then 1
     | y % 3 == 0 then 2
     | y % 5 == 0 then 3
     else 7
negative_example:
if y % 2 == 0 then 1 | y % 3 == 0 then 2   // missing final else

title: Numeric type parameters can be demoted to values using backticks
rule: A finite numeric type expression can be turned into a value using backtick notation such as `` `n ``. This is syntax sugar for `number` applied to the type argument.
rationale: Demotion is essential when code needs the value corresponding to a type-level natural.
positive_example:
`t
number`{t}
negative_example: n    // using a type parameter as though it were already a value
constraints: The demoted type expression must be finite and numeric.
applicable_when: relating width parameters to runtime arithmetic

`t

title: Tuples and records package values using different constructors
rule: Tuples are enclosed in parentheses and identified by position, while records are enclosed in braces and identified by named fields.
rationale: Generated code should choose tuples or records deliberately because projection and equality behavior differ.
constraints: Tuple components are expressions.
Record components are `label = value` pairs.

retrieval_text:

topic: basic_types
subtopic: records

rule: Tuples are enclosed in parentheses and identified by position, while records are enclosed in braces and identified by named fields.
constraints: Tuple components are expressions. | Record components are `label = value` pairs.
applicable_when: packaging multiple outputs | encoding structured state
positive_example:
(1,2,3)
{ x = 1, y = 2 }
negative_example:
{ 1, 2 }
(x = 1, y = 2)

title: Explicit record selectors require sufficient type information
rule: Explicit tuple and record selectors such as `.0` or `.x` may be used only when there is enough type information to determine the shape of the tuple or record.
rationale: Selectors on unconstrained values are ambiguous and rejected.
constraints: Pattern matching is an alternative way to access components when explicit selectors are inconvenient.

constraints: Pattern matching is an alternative way to access components when explicit selectors are inconvenient.
applicable_when: accessing record fields | projecting tuple components
positive_example:
type T = { sign : Bit, number : [15] }
isPositive : T -> Bit
isPositive x = x.sign
negative_example:
badDef x = x.f

title: Tuple and record components can be updated with record-update syntax
rule: Use record-update syntax to set or transform tuple and record fields, including nested fields.
rationale: Field updates are a compact, idiomatic way to modify structured data.
constraints: Nested updates are allowed.
Relative updates use `->` to reference the old field value.

applicable_when: rewriting state records | updating tuple or record outputs
positive_example:
{ r | x = 30 }
{ r | x -> x + 5 }
{ n | pt.x = 10 }
negative_example:
r.x = 30

title: Finite sequences use type `[n] a` and words abbreviate `[n] Bit`
rule: A finite sequence of length `n` with elements of type `a` has type `[n] a`. A finite sequence of bits `[n] Bit` may be abbreviated as `[n]`.
rationale: Width-indexed sequences are central to Cryptol modeling and must be typed precisely.
constraints: Infinite sequences use `[inf] a`.

constraints: Infinite sequences use `[inf] a`.
applicable_when: encoding bitvectors | writing arrays or matrices | modeling words and byte arrays
positive_example:
[8] Bit
[256][12]
[32]
negative_example:
Bit[32]

title: Finite sequence bounds in enumerations are type expressions
rule: In finite sequence enumerations using `..`, the bounds are type expressions; in infinite sequences, the bounds are value expressions.
rationale: This distinction matters when generating bounded vs. streaming sequences.
negative_example: Assume finite and infinite sequence bounds follow the same typing rules.
constraints: Finite enumerations and infinite streams use related but distinct syntax forms.
applicable_when: choosing between bounded and unbounded sequence forms

positive_example:
[t1 .. t2]
[e1 ...]
negative_example:
Assume finite and infinite sequence bounds follow the same typing rules.

title: Sequences support construction, indexing, slicing, and split patterns
rule: Common sequence operators include concatenation `#`, indexing `@` and `!`, slicing `@@` and `!!`, and split patterns such as `p1 # p2`.
rationale: These operators are among the most common sequence-manipulation forms in Cryptol code.
constraints: Use front/back variants according to whether indexing is from the start or end.

constraints: Use front/back variants according to whether indexing is from the start or end.
applicable_when: translating byte/bit extraction logic | splitting and joining words
positive_example:
xs @ i
xs @@ [0 .. 3]
p1 # p2
negative_example:
xs[i]

title: Functions are defined with pattern parameters or lambdas
rule: Use `f p1 p2 = e` for named function definitions and `\p1 p2 -> e` for lambdas. Parameters may be patterns.
rationale: Pattern parameters are common in Cryptol and interact naturally with tuples and records.
positive_example:
\p1 p2 -> e
f p1 p2 = e
constraints: Pattern forms must themselves be legal Cryptol patterns.

applicable_when: generating helpers | writing higher-order code | destructuring tuple inputs
positive_example:
\p1 p2 -> e
f p1 p2 = e
negative_example:
function f(x) = e

title: Type synonyms are transparent aliases and cannot be recursive
rule: A `type` declaration creates a transparent alias for an existing type expression. Type synonyms may mention other synonyms but may not form recursive groups.
rationale: Type synonyms improve readability but do not create distinct types.
positive_example: type T a b = [a] b
constraints: Type synonyms are unfolded at use sites.

constraints: Type synonyms are unfolded at use sites.
applicable_when: naming complex widths | defining reusable aliases
positive_example:
type T a b = [a] b
negative_example:
type T = T

title: Newtypes create distinct named types with record bodies
rule: A `newtype` declaration defines a distinct named type whose body is a record. Unlike a type synonym, a newtype is treated as a separate type by the type checker.
rationale: Choosing `newtype` vs `type` changes equality, instance derivation, and constructor use.
positive_example: newtype NewT a b = { seq : [a] b }
negative_example: Assume `newtype` behaves exactly like `type` at all use sites.
constraints: Every newtype introduces a constructor with the same name.
Newtypes may not form recursive groups.
applicable_when: wrapping structured values with stronger type distinctions

title: Newtypes create distinct named types with record bodies
topic: type_declarations
subtopic: newtypes
keywords: newtype | record body | distinct type
rule: A `newtype` declaration defines a distinct named type whose body is a record. Unlike a type synonym, a newtype is treated as a separate type by the type checker.
constraints: Every newtype introduces a constructor with the same name. | Newtypes may not form recursive groups.
applicable_when: wrapping structured values with stronger type distinctions | adding derived instances deliberately
positive_example:
newtype NewT a b = { seq : [a] b }
negative_example:
Assume `newtype` behaves exactly like `type` at all use sites.

title: Newtype values are built with constructors and projected like records
rule: A `newtype` declaration brings a constructor with the same name into scope, and record-style field projection may be used on values of the newtype body.
rationale: This affects how wrapped data is constructed and accessed in generated code.
negative_example: x = { seq = [1,2,3] }   // missing constructor for a newtype value
constraints: Projection works on fields of the underlying record body.
retrieval_title: type_declarations / newtypes / Newtype values are built with constructors and projected like records
retrieval_text:
title: Newtype values are built with constructors and projected like records
topic: type_declarations
subtopic: newtypes
keywords: newtype constructor | projection
rule: A `newtype` declaration brings a constructor with the same name into scope, and record-style field projection may be used on values of the newtype body.
constraints: Projection works on fields of the underlying record body.
applicable_when: constructing newtype values | reading fields from wrapped data
positive_example:
x : NewT 3 Integer
x = NewT { seq = [1,2,3] }

sum x.seq
negative_example:
x = { seq = [1,2,3] }   // missing constructor for a newtype value

title: Enums define algebraic data types with uppercase constructors
rule: An `enum` declaration defines a distinct algebraic data type using one or more constructors. Constructor names must start with an uppercase letter.
rationale: Constructor naming is both a syntactic and semantic distinction in `case` patterns.
positive_example: enum Maybe a = Nothing | Just a
negative_example: enum Maybe a = nothing | just a
constraints: Constructors may have zero or more fields.
Enums are distinct named types.
retrieval_title: type_declarations / enums / Enums define algebraic data types with uppercase constructors
retrieval_text:
title: Enums define algebraic data types with uppercase constructors
topic: type_declarations
subtopic: enums
keywords: enum | constructor | uppercase
rule: An `enum` declaration defines a distinct algebraic data type using one or more constructors. Constructor names must start with an uppercase letter.
constraints: Constructors may have zero or more fields. | Enums are distinct named types.
applicable_when: modeling optional or tagged results | generating ADTs for partial functions
positive_example:
enum Maybe a = Nothing | Just a
negative_example:
enum Maybe a = nothing | just a

title: Case expressions are the way to inspect enum values
rule: Use `case e of ...` to examine an enum value. Constructor patterns may bind fields, and a plain variable or `_` may be used as a catch-all branch.
rationale: Enums are not inspected with ordinary record/tuple selectors.
positive_example:
case e of
  Nothing -> 0
  Just a  -> a + 1
constraints: The matched expression must have a known enum type.
retrieval_title: type_declarations / case_expressions / Case expressions are the way to inspect enum values
retrieval_text:
title: Case expressions are the way to inspect enum values
topic: type_declarations
subtopic: case_expressions
keywords: case | enum | pattern matching
rule: Use `case e of ...` to examine an enum value. Constructor patterns may bind fields, and a plain variable or `_` may be used as a catch-all branch.
constraints: The matched expression must have a known enum type.
applicable_when: handling Option/Result | branching on tagged values
positive_example:
case e of
  Nothing -> 0
  Just a  -> a + 1
negative_example:
if e == Just a then ...

title: Case patterns must be exhaustive and non-overlapping
rule: Patterns in a `case` expression must cover all constructors of the enum and must be disjoint. A catch-all clause, if used, must occur last.
rationale: Pattern coverage and overlap are checked statically in Cryptol.
positive_example:
isNothing x =
  case x of
    Nothing -> True
    _       -> False
negative_example:
case x of
  Nothing -> True

case x of
  Just _  -> False
  _       -> True
  Nothing -> False
constraints: No two branches may match the same constructor.
Catch-all clauses must be last.
retrieval_title: type_declarations / case_expressions / Case patterns must be exhaustive and non-overlapping
retrieval_text:
title: Case patterns must be exhaustive and non-overlapping
topic: type_declarations
subtopic: case_expressions
keywords: case | exhaustive | overlapping | _
rule: Patterns in a `case` expression must cover all constructors of the enum and must be disjoint. A catch-all clause, if used, must occur last.
constraints: No two branches may match the same constructor. | Catch-all clauses must be last.
applicable_when: writing case branches for enums | repairing coverage errors
positive_example:
isNothing x =
  case x of
    Nothing -> True
    _       -> False
negative_example:
case x of
  Nothing -> True

case x of
  Just _  -> False
  _       -> True
  Nothing -> False

title: Each file may contain at most one top-level module
rule: A top-level Cryptol file may contain at most one top-level module, written as `module M where`.
rationale: This is the basic unit of file-level organization in Cryptol.
positive_example:
module M where

type T = [8]

f : [8]
f = 10
negative_example:
module A where
...
module B where
...
constraints: Submodules are allowed, but they are nested inside a containing module.
retrieval_title: modules / module_declarations / Each file may contain at most one top-level module

topic: modules
subtopic: module_declarations
keywords: module | top-level | where
rule: A top-level Cryptol file may contain at most one top-level module, written as `module M where`.
constraints: Submodules are allowed, but they are nested inside a containing module.
applicable_when: creating new files | emitting file headers
positive_example:
module M where

type T = [8]

f : [8]
f = 10
negative_example:
module A where
...
module B where
...

title: Hierarchical module names use `::` and map to directory structure
rule: A module name may be hierarchical, using `::` between identifiers, such as `Hash::SHA256`. The module loader uses this structure to locate the file in the directory tree.
rationale: Hierarchical names support namespace organization across larger projects.
positive_example: module Hash::SHA256 where
constraints: Avoid using two consecutive underscores in module names because those are used for anonymous module names.
retrieval_title: modules / hierarchical_names / Hierarchical module names use `::` and map to directory structure
retrieval_text:
title: Hierarchical module names use `::` and map to directory structure
topic: modules
subtopic: hierarchical_names
keywords: hierarchical modules | :: | CRYPTOLPATH
rule: A module name may be hierarchical, using `::` between identifiers, such as `Hash::SHA256`. The module loader uses this structure to locate the file in the directory tree.
constraints: Avoid using two consecutive underscores in module names because those are used for anonymous module names.
applicable_when: designing project structure | generating multi-file libraries
positive_example:
module Hash::SHA256 where
negative_example:
module Hash/SHA256 where

title: Import declarations bring module definitions into scope
rule: Use `import M` to bring exported definitions from module `M` into scope.
rationale: Imports are the standard mechanism for cross-module reuse.
positive_example:
module N where
import M

g = f
negative_example:
module N where
use M

g = f
constraints: Imported names come from the exported interface of the referenced module.
retrieval_title: modules / imports / Import declarations bring module definitions into scope
retrieval_text:

topic: modules
subtopic: imports
keywords: import | scope | module
rule: Use `import M` to bring exported definitions from module `M` into scope.
constraints: Imported names come from the exported interface of the referenced module.
applicable_when: wiring together multiple modules | reusing helpers from other files
positive_example:
module N where
import M

g = f
negative_example:
module N where
use M

g = f

title: Import lists restrict which names are imported
rule: Use an import list such as `import M(f,g)` to import only selected names from a module.
rationale: Explicit import lists reduce collisions and make dependency sources clearer.
positive_example:
import M(f,g)

x = f + g
constraints: Names omitted from the list are not imported.
retrieval_title: modules / import_lists / Import lists restrict which names are imported

topic: modules
subtopic: import_lists
keywords: import list | import M(f,g)
rule: Use an import list such as `import M(f,g)` to import only selected names from a module.
constraints: Names omitted from the list are not imported.
applicable_when: minimizing imported namespace | documenting dependency usage clearly
positive_example:
import M(f,g)

x = f + g
negative_example:
import M(f g)

title: Hiding imports exclude selected names from an otherwise full import
rule: Use `import M hiding (h)` to import everything from `M` except the named exclusions.
rationale: This is convenient when only a small number of names would cause collisions.
positive_example:
import M hiding (h)

x = f + g
constraints: Hiding is especially useful when a module exports many names.
applicable_when: avoiding a small number of import conflicts
keeping most names from a dependency
retrieval_title: modules / hiding_imports / Hiding imports exclude selected names from an otherwise full import
retrieval_text:

subtopic: hiding_imports

constraints: Hiding is especially useful when a module exports many names.
applicable_when: avoiding a small number of import conflicts | keeping most names from a dependency
positive_example:
import M hiding (h)

x = f + g
negative_example:
import M except (h)

title: Qualified imports require qualified references
rule: After `import M as P`, imported definitions should be referenced with the qualifier, for example `P::f`.
rationale: Qualified imports avoid name collisions and make symbol origin explicit.
positive_example:
module N where
import M as P

g = P::f
negative_example:
module N where
import M as P

g = f
constraints: Qualified imports may be combined with import lists or hiding clauses.
applicable_when: making module provenance explicit in generated code

negative_example:

title: Private blocks hide helper declarations from other modules
rule: Use a `private` block to declare helper definitions that should not be visible outside the current module.
rationale: Private blocks are a module-organization tool that helps separate public API from internal helpers.
positive_example:
module M where

f : [8]
f = 0x01 + helper1 + helper2

private
  helper1 : [8]
  helper1 = 2

  helper2 : [8]
  helper2 = 3
constraints: `private` introduces a new layout scope.
A module may contain multiple private blocks.

applicable_when: hiding internal helpers | separating public and private declarations
positive_example:
module M where

f : [8]
f = 0x01 + helper1 + helper2

private
  helper1 : [8]
  helper1 = 2

  helper2 : [8]
  helper2 = 3
negative_example:
private helper1 = 2   // not a block

title: Submodules are declared with `submodule` and can capture outer scope names
rule: A nested module is declared with `submodule N where`. A submodule may refer to names from its enclosing scope and may later be imported with `import submodule N`.
rationale: Submodules support local modular structure without creating separate files.
positive_example:
module M where

  x = 0x02

  submodule N where
    y = x + 2

  import submodule N as P

  z = 2 * P::y
negative_example:
module M where
  module N where
    y = x + 2
constraints: Declarations in a submodule shadow outer names.
Recursive definitions across modules are not allowed.

subtopic: submodules
keywords: submodule | nested modules | import submodule
rule: A nested module is declared with `submodule N where`. A submodule may refer to names from its enclosing scope and may later be imported with `import submodule N`.
constraints: Declarations in a submodule shadow outer names. | Recursive definitions across modules are not allowed.
applicable_when: organizing nested helper namespaces | encapsulating related local definitions
positive_example:
module M where

  x = 0x02

  submodule N where
    y = x + 2

  import submodule N as P

  z = 2 * P::y
negative_example:
module M where
  module N where
    y = x + 2

title: Parameter blocks create anonymous module interfaces for functor-like modules
rule: A `parameter` block defines module parameters, such as numeric types, constraints, and values, for a parameterized module.
rationale: This is the concise syntax for creating functor-like modules without declaring a separate interface module explicitly.
positive_example:
module M where

  parameter
    type n : #
    type constraint (fin n, n >= 1)
    x : [n]

  f : [n]
  f = 1 + x
constraints: A parameter block may contain the same kinds of declarations that interfaces may contain.
retrieval_title: modules / parameter_blocks / Parameter blocks create anonymous module interfaces for functor-like modules
retrieval_text:

subtopic: parameter_blocks
keywords: parameter | interface | module parameters
rule: A `parameter` block defines module parameters, such as numeric types, constraints, and values, for a parameterized module.
constraints: A parameter block may contain the same kinds of declarations that interfaces may contain.
applicable_when: building reusable parameterized modules | defining width-parameterized components
positive_example:
module M where

  parameter
    type n : #
    type constraint (fin n, n >= 1)
    x : [n]

  f : [n]
  f = 1 + x
negative_example:
parameter x : [8] = 1

title: Parameterized modules are instantiated by providing module arguments
rule: A parameterized module may be instantiated by defining a new module equal to the functor and supplying bindings for its parameters, either via existing modules or a `where` body.
rationale: Instantiation is how abstract module parameters become concrete definitions.
positive_example:
module Impl where
  type n = 8
  x = 26

module MyF = F { Impl }
constraints: When multiple interfaces are imported, instantiation arguments are matched by parameter name.
Anonymous `where` instantiations are supported.
applicable_when: building concrete variants of generic specs
retrieval_title: modules / module_instantiation / Parameterized modules are instantiated by providing module arguments

topic: modules
subtopic: module_instantiation
keywords: module instantiation | = | { Impl } | where
rule: A parameterized module may be instantiated by defining a new module equal to the functor and supplying bindings for its parameters, either via existing modules or a `where` body.
constraints: When multiple interfaces are imported, instantiation arguments are matched by parameter name. | Anonymous `where` instantiations are supported.
applicable_when: materializing parameterized modules | building concrete variants of generic specs
positive_example:
module Impl where
  type n = 8
  x = 26

module MyF = F { Impl }
negative_example:
module MyF = F(Impl)

title: Finite sequence enumeration bounds must be type expressions, not value expressions
rule: In a finite enumeration [a .. b], both a and b must be numeric TYPE expressions. Value-level expressions including backtick-demoted types are NOT allowed.
rationale: Most common parse error in generated Cryptol. Use take`{n} [0 ...] or [0 .. (n-1)] instead.
positive_example:
[0 .. 7]          // literal: OK
[0 .. (n-1)]      // type arithmetic: OK
take`{n} [0 ...]  // take: OK
negative_example:
[0 .. `n - 1]     // WRONG: value expression
[0 .. (`n - 1)]   // WRONG: still value
constraints: Backtick converts type to value, ineligible as bound.
retrieval_title: basic_types / sequences / Finite sequence enumeration bounds must be type expressions, not value expressions

subtopic: sequences

rule: In a finite enumeration [a .. b], both a and b must be numeric TYPE expressions. Value-level expressions including backtick-demoted types are NOT allowed.
constraints: Backtick converts type to value, ineligible as bound.
applicable_when: generating type-parameterized length sequences | fixing boundaries of .. sequences errors
positive_example:
[0 .. 7]          // literal: OK
[0 .. (n-1)]      // type arithmetic: OK
take`{n} [0 ...]  // take: OK
negative_example:
[0 .. `n - 1]     // WRONG: value expression
[0 .. (`n - 1)]   // WRONG: still value

title: div and mod are valid built-in Cryptol operators
rule: Cryptol provides div and mod as built-in operators. / is equivalent to div, % is equivalent to mod. These are valid and commonly used.
rationale: div and mod are standard Cryptol arithmetic operators, NOT Haskell-only imports.
positive_example:
x % q
result = (a + b) % 3329
quot = x / 2
negative_example: // Do NOT avoid div/mod thinking they are unsupported
constraints: Require Ring and Integral type classes.
Division by zero is undefined.

constraints: Require Ring and Integral type classes. | Division by zero is undefined.
applicable_when: modular arithmetic | integer division in bit decomposition
positive_example:
x % q
result = (a + b) % 3329
quot = x / 2
negative_example:
// Do NOT avoid div/mod thinking they are unsupported

title: Comprehension generator variables are scoped only inside that comprehension
rule: A variable introduced by a comprehension generator is available only inside that comprehension’s element expression and its qualifier list. It is not automatically visible in sibling declarations or a trailing `where` block.
rationale: Generator bindings are lexical and local. Referring to them from a sibling helper often causes `Value not in scope` errors.
negative_example:
xs = [ base + j | j <- [0 .. 7] ]
  where
    base = j * 2
constraints: If a helper needs the generator-bound variable, pass it as an explicit argument or compute the value inline.
applicable_when: repairing `Value not in scope` near comprehension code
retrieval_title: expressions / comprehension_scope / Comprehension generator variables are scoped only inside that comprehension
retrieval_text:
title: Comprehension generator variables are scoped only inside that comprehension
topic: expressions
subtopic: comprehension_scope
keywords: comprehension | generator | scope | | | <- | where | not in scope
rule: A variable introduced by a comprehension generator is available only inside that comprehension’s element expression and its qualifier list. It is not automatically visible in sibling declarations or a trailing `where` block.
constraints: If a helper needs the generator-bound variable, pass it as an explicit argument or compute the value inline.
applicable_when: writing comprehensions with helper calculations | repairing `Value not in scope` near comprehension code
positive_example:
pairs = [ (i, i + 1) | i <- [0 .. 7] ]
negative_example:
xs = [ base + j | j <- [0 .. 7] ]
  where
    base = j * 2

title: Sibling `where` bindings do not capture lambda or comprehension-bound names implicitly
rule: A local binding in a `where` block may use names from the function’s outer scope, but it does not implicitly capture variables introduced only inside a sibling lambda, pattern, or comprehension generator.
rationale: This prevents accidental use of short-lived local names outside the place they are bound.
positive_example:
f xs = [ g i | i <- xs ]
  where
    g i = i + 1
negative_example:
f xs = [ h | i <- xs ]
  where
    h = i + 1
constraints: Turn the dependent helper into a function, for example `g i = ...`.
applicable_when: factoring helper expressions out of a comprehension
moving computations into a where block
retrieval_title: expressions / local_declarations / Sibling `where` bindings do not capture lambda or comprehension-bound names implicitly

constraints: Turn the dependent helper into a function, for example `g i = ...`.
applicable_when: factoring helper expressions out of a comprehension | moving computations into a where block
positive_example:

  where
    g i = i + 1
negative_example:

  where
    h = i + 1

title: Backtick demotion is only for numeric type parameters or numeric type expressions
rule: Use backticks only to demote a numeric type parameter or numeric type expression to a term-level number. Do not put backticks on ordinary term variables or arbitrary value expressions.
rationale: Backticks bridge type-level numerics to values; they are not a general quoting operator.
positive_example:
length : {n, a} (fin n, Literal n Integer) => [n]a -> Integer
length _ = `n
constraints: A term variable such as `i` should be used directly without backticks.
applicable_when: using numeric type parameters in value arithmetic
repairing `Expected a value named ... but found a type instead`
retrieval_title: expressions / type_demotion / Backtick demotion is only for numeric type parameters or numeric type expressions

subtopic: type_demotion
keywords: backtick | `n | number | type demotion | type parameter | value variable

constraints: A term variable such as `i` should be used directly without backticks.
applicable_when: using numeric type parameters in value arithmetic | repairing `Expected a value named ... but found a type instead`
positive_example:
length : {n, a} (fin n, Literal n Integer) => [n]a -> Integer
length _ = `n
negative_example:
idx = `i
sum = `(x + 1)

title: If-expressions contain expressions, not assignment-style statement sequences
rule: Each branch of an `if ... then ... else ...` must be a single expression. If a branch needs intermediate names, use a nested `where` or `let`; do not write assignment-style lines under `else` as though Cryptol were imperative.
rationale: Cryptol conditionals are expressions, so statement-like branch bodies lead to parse errors.
positive_example:
f x = if x == 0 then 0 else out
  where
    out = x + 1
negative_example:
f x = if x == 0 then 0 else
  y = x + 1
  y
constraints: Prefer `where` for multi-step branch logic when readability matters.
retrieval_title: expressions / conditionals / If-expressions contain expressions, not assignment-style statement sequences
retrieval_text:

subtopic: conditionals

rule: Each branch of an `if ... then ... else ...` must be a single expression. If a branch needs intermediate names, use a nested `where` or `let`; do not write assignment-style lines under `else` as though Cryptol were imperative.
constraints: Prefer `where` for multi-step branch logic when readability matters.
applicable_when: translating imperative branch bodies | repairing parse errors near `unexpected =` inside `else`
positive_example:
f x = if x == 0 then 0 else out
  where
    out = x + 1
negative_example:
f x = if x == 0 then 0 else
  y = x + 1
  y

title: Sequences are homogeneous; use tuples or records for heterogeneous fixed-size state
rule: A Cryptol sequence `[n]a` contains `n` elements of the same type `a`. If a small state bundles fields of different types, represent it as a tuple or record, not as a sequence literal.
rationale: Using a sequence for heterogeneous state causes cascading type mismatches when Cryptol tries to unify all elements to one type.
positive_example:
state : ([256][12], [8])
state = (arr, idx)
constraints: Use sequences only when every element truly has the same type.
retrieval_title: basic_types / sequences / Sequences are homogeneous; use tuples or records for heterogeneous fixed-size state

keywords: sequence | homogeneous | tuple | record | state | heterogeneous | [x,y]
rule: A Cryptol sequence `[n]a` contains `n` elements of the same type `a`. If a small state bundles fields of different types, represent it as a tuple or record, not as a sequence literal.
constraints: Use sequences only when every element truly has the same type.
applicable_when: threading loop state | translating imperative accumulators | repairing mixed-type state errors
positive_example:
state : ([256][12], [8])
state = (arr, idx)
negative_example:
state = [arr, idx]

title: Shape-oriented operators such as `take`, `drop`, and `split` rely on type-level size information
rule: Common shape operators such as `take`, `drop`, and `split` are usually resolved by type-level size information or result context. Do not assume an arbitrary runtime count can always be used to specialize an infinite sequence into a finite one.
rationale: When the intended result length is not known at the type level, these operators often become ambiguous or ill-typed.
positive_example:
prefix : {n} (fin n) => [inf]Integer -> [n]Integer
prefix xs = take`{n} xs
constraints: If the count is genuinely runtime-dependent, prefer value-level recursion or a different representation.
applicable_when: generating finite sequences from open-ended streams
retrieval_title: basic_types / sequences / Shape-oriented operators such as `take`, `drop`, and `split` rely on type-level size information

subtopic: sequences

rule: Common shape operators such as `take`, `drop`, and `split` are usually resolved by type-level size information or result context. Do not assume an arbitrary runtime count can always be used to specialize an infinite sequence into a finite one.
constraints: If the count is genuinely runtime-dependent, prefer value-level recursion or a different representation.
applicable_when: generating finite sequences from open-ended streams | repairing `Ambiguous numeric type` around `take` or `split`
positive_example:
prefix : {n} (fin n) => [inf]Integer -> [n]Integer
prefix xs = take`{n} xs
negative_example:
ys = take count [0 ...]

title: Comments do not place names in scope
rule: A name is in scope only if it is built-in, imported, passed as a parameter, or defined in the module. Mentioning a dependency in a comment does not make the name available.
rationale: Generated code often annotates 'assumed defined elsewhere' while still referencing an undefined name; this remains a scope error.
positive_example:
import M as P
x = P::f y
negative_example:
// assume f is provided elsewhere
x = f y
constraints: When a dependency is unavailable, either import it properly or produce a conservative unresolved skeleton.
applicable_when: repairing `Value not in scope` for commented dependencies
retrieval_title: modules / imports / Comments do not place names in scope
retrieval_text:
title: Comments do not place names in scope
topic: modules
subtopic: imports
keywords: scope | import | dependency | comment | not in scope | assumed defined
rule: A name is in scope only if it is built-in, imported, passed as a parameter, or defined in the module. Mentioning a dependency in a comment does not make the name available.
constraints: When a dependency is unavailable, either import it properly or produce a conservative unresolved skeleton.
applicable_when: using external primitives | repairing `Value not in scope` for commented dependencies
positive_example:
import M as P
x = P::f y
negative_example:
// assume f is provided elsewhere
x = f y
